annotations.label: Ball (solid figure)

anchor.snippet: a ball is the solid figure bounded by a sphere

annotations.note: Mathlib defines metric balls (Metric.ball / Metric.closedBall) rather than the geometric solid bounded by a sphere; the informal notion is captured up to generalization.

annotations.label: Closed vs open ball

anchor.snippet: It may be a closed ball (including the boundary points that constitute the sphere) or an open ball

annotations.note: Mathlib has both Metric.ball (strict) and Metric.closedBall (≤) in the same file, distinguishing open and closed balls.

annotations.label: n-ball / hyperball

anchor.snippet: A ball in n dimensions is called a hyperball or n -ball

annotations.note: There is no dedicated `n-ball` notion; one takes Metric.ball in EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n) so the dimensional terminology is implicit.

annotations.label: Open Euclidean n-ball

anchor.snippet: an (open) n -ball of radius r and center x is the set of all points of distance less than r from x

annotations.note: Metric.ball x r := {y | dist y x < r} specializes to the Euclidean open n-ball when applied to EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n).

annotations.label: Closed Euclidean n-ball

anchor.snippet: A closed n -ball of radius r is the set of all points of distance less than or equal to r away from x

annotations.note: Metric.closedBall x r := {y | dist y x ≤ r}, which is the closed Euclidean n-ball when used in EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n).

annotations.label: Euclidean ball bounded by hypersphere

anchor.snippet: every ball is bounded by a hypersphere

annotations.note: Mathlib defines Metric.sphere as the dist = r locus and has frontier/closure lemmas, but no single named result phrased as `the boundary of the ball is the sphere`.

annotations.label: Volume of Euclidean n-ball

anchor.snippet: The n -dimensional volume of a Euclidean ball of radius r

annotations.note: EuclideanSpace.volume_ball gives the volume of Metric.ball x r in EuclideanSpace ℝ ι as rⁿ · π^(n/2)/Γ(n/2+1).

annotations.label: Double factorial

anchor.snippet: the double factorial (2 k + 1)!! is defined for odd integers

annotations.note: Nat.doubleFactorial defines n!! by the standard recursion, covering both parities of n.

annotations.label: Surface area of n-ball

anchor.snippet: The surface area of an n-ball

annotations.note: Loogle finds no `volume_sphere`/`areaOfSphere`/`sphere_surface` decl giving the (n-1)-dimensional Hausdorff measure of the Euclidean sphere.

annotations.label: Open metric ball

anchor.snippet: The open (metric) ball of radius r centered at a point p in M

annotations.note: Metric.ball is exactly the open ball in any (pseudo)metric space.

annotations.label: Closed metric ball

anchor.snippet: The closed (metric) ball, sometimes denoted

annotations.note: Metric.closedBall is the closed ball in any (pseudo)metric space.

annotations.label: Unit ball

anchor.snippet: A unit ball (open or closed) is a ball of radius 1

annotations.note: Mathlib uses `Metric.ball 0 1` / `Metric.closedBall 0 1` for the unit ball (e.g. via Homeomorph.unitBall in Mathlib.Analysis.Normed.Module.Ball.Homeomorph).

annotations.label: Chebyshev and taxicab balls

anchor.snippet: a ball in real coordinate space under the Chebyshev distance is a hypercube

annotations.note: Mathlib has PiLp / L^p norms (including L^1 and L^∞) so the balls exist, but the geometric identification with hypercubes/octahedra is not explicitly stated.

annotations.label: Closed ball in infinite-dim space is not compact

anchor.snippet: a closed ball in any infinite-dimensional normed vector space is never compact

annotations.note: FiniteDimensional.of_isCompact_closedBall is Riesz's theorem: compactness of a positive-radius closed ball forces finite-dimensionality, equivalent to the contrapositive.

annotations.label: Balls in normed spaces are convex

anchor.snippet: a ball in a normed vector space will always be convex

annotations.note: convex_ball states `Convex ℝ (Metric.ball a r)` in any real seminormed space.

annotations.label: Bounded subset of metric space

anchor.snippet: A subset of a metric space is bounded if it is contained in some ball

annotations.note: Bornology.IsBounded is the bornology-bounded predicate, and Bornology.IsBounded.subset_ball confirms it is equivalent to containment in some metric ball.

annotations.label: Totally bounded set

anchor.snippet: A set is totally bounded if, given any positive radius

annotations.note: TotallyBounded is defined in uniform spaces and specializes to the standard finite-cover-by-balls notion in metric spaces.

annotations.label: Topology induced by a metric

anchor.snippet: The open balls of a metric space can serve as a base

annotations.note: Metric.isOpen_iff characterizes open sets via open balls, witnessing that the open balls form a base of the metric topology.

annotations.label: Closure of open ball under discrete metric

anchor.snippet: denote the closure of the open ball

annotations.note: No specific lemma comparing closure(Metric.ball) with Metric.closedBall under the discrete metric was found via loogle.

annotations.label: Balls in a normed vector space

anchor.snippet: Any normed vector space V with norm

annotations.note: In a normed space, Metric.ball/closedBall are exactly the norm balls since dist is induced by the norm.

annotations.label: Open ball in p-norm

anchor.snippet: In a Cartesian space R n with the p -norm L p

annotations.note: PiLp p α equips ∏ α with the L^p norm; Metric.ball on PiLp is the p-norm open ball.

annotations.label: p-norm balls in the plane

anchor.snippet: according to the L 1 -norm (often called the taxicab or Manhattan metric) are bounded by squares

annotations.note: No Mathlib decl identifies the L¹/L∞ unit balls in ℝ² with explicit squares.

annotations.label: p-norm balls in 3-space

anchor.snippet: the L 1 -balls are within octahedra with axes-aligned body diagonals

annotations.note: No Mathlib decl identifies the L¹/L∞ unit balls in ℝ³ with octahedra/cubes.

annotations.label: Infinity-norm ball

anchor.snippet: Often can also consider the case of

annotations.note: PiLp ∞ gives the sup norm and its Metric.ball is the L^∞ ball.

annotations.label: Norm from centrally symmetric convex set

anchor.snippet: given any centrally symmetric , bounded , open , and convex subset X of R n , one can define a norm

annotations.note: gaugeSeminorm constructs a seminorm from a balanced convex absorbent set (the Minkowski functional), exactly the claim with `centrally symmetric, bounded, open, convex` hypotheses translated.

annotations.label: Topological n-ball

anchor.snippet: An (open or closed) n -dimensional topological ball of X is any subset of X which is homeomorphic

annotations.note: No declaration of `topological n-ball` (a subset homeomorphic to a Euclidean ball) was found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Open topological n-ball homeomorphic to R^n

anchor.snippet: Any open topological n -ball is homeomorphic to the Cartesian space R n

annotations.note: Homeomorph.unitBall gives E ≃ₜ Metric.ball (0:E) 1, providing the ℝⁿ ≃ open unit ball case but not the general statement about arbitrary topological n-balls.

annotations.label: Closed topological n-ball homeomorphic to n-cube

anchor.snippet: Any closed topological n -ball is homeomorphic to the closed n -cube

annotations.note: No homeomorphism between Mathlib's closed Euclidean ball and a closed n-cube was located.

annotations.label: n-ball homeomorphic to m-ball iff n=m

anchor.snippet: An n -ball is homeomorphic to an m -ball if and only if n = m

annotations.note: Mathlib has no invariance-of-dimension result distinguishing Euclidean balls of different dimensions.

annotations.label: Topological balls need not be smooth

anchor.snippet: A topological n -ball need not be smooth

annotations.note: No Mathlib decl on exotic smooth structures on topological balls was found.

annotations.label: Ball regions (cap, segment, shell, wedge)

anchor.snippet: A number of special regions can be defined for a ball

annotations.note: Spherical caps, segments, shells, and wedges are not defined in Mathlib.
